questions.q: A mutex (mutual exclusion lock) ensures that at most one thread can execute a critical section at a time. Which statement about mutex behavior is correct?
options: Mutexes automatically release when a thread's time slice ends
A mutex prevents thread starvation by guaranteeing fair scheduling across all waiting threads
A thread that acquires a mutex must release it; if released by a different thread, behavior is typically undefined
Recursive mutexes allow the same thread to acquire the lock unlimited times without deadlock
questions.explanation: Mutexes are not automatically released at context switches—the owning thread must explicitly release. While a thread can acquire a recursive mutex multiple times, it must release it the same number of times. Only the thread that acquired a mutex should release it; other threads doing so is undefined behavior.

questions.q: A semaphore with initial count N can be used to manage a pool of N identical resources. After one thread calls wait() and another calls signal(), what is the new semaphore count?
options: N
The final value depends on the order and timing of calls
questions.explanation: Each wait() decrements the semaphore count by 1, and each signal() increments it by 1. Starting from N, one wait decrements to N-1, then one signal increments to N. The order does matter—if signal() runs first, it reaches N+1, then wait() brings it to N, so the answer is deterministic.

questions.q: A condition variable typically works with a mutex. When a thread calls wait() on a condition variable, what happens to the associated mutex?
options: The mutex is destroyed and recreated when the thread wakes up
The mutex is atomically released so other threads can access the critical section; it is reacquired when the thread wakes up
The mutex remains locked, preventing other threads from entering the critical section
The mutex is converted to a read-write lock
questions.explanation: A condition variable's wait() method atomically releases the mutex and puts the thread to sleep. When signaled, the thread wakes and reacquires the mutex before resuming. This prevents a race where the condition is notified between the lock release and the sleep. The mutex is never destroyed or converted.

questions.q: A barrier synchronization primitive blocks all N threads until all N threads have reached the barrier. After all threads pass the barrier and it resets, what is the key invariant maintained?
options: The barrier remains locked until a supervisor thread unlocks it
The next barrier cycle will again block all N threads until all reach it
Once a barrier is passed, subsequent barriers are non-blocking for efficiency
Threads must call reset() explicitly before using the barrier again
questions.explanation: A reusable barrier resets automatically after all threads pass. It then re-enters a blocking state waiting for all N threads to reach it again. This maintains the invariant that the barrier synchronizes every batch of N threads sequentially. Implicit reset ensures threads don't accidentally skip the next synchronization point.

questions.q: In a monitor (synchronized object), both method entry and exit are protected by a lock. If thread A holds the monitor lock and calls wait() on a condition variable, which statement is true?
options: Thread A sleeps while holding the lock, blocking all other threads from entering monitor methods
Thread A releases the lock, allowing other threads to enter monitor methods and potentially signal the condition
All other threads are blocked indefinitely until A explicitly releases the lock
The monitor is temporarily converted to a read-only mode
questions.explanation: When A calls wait() on a condition variable inside a monitor method, it atomically releases the monitor lock. Other threads can now acquire the lock and call other monitor methods or signal the condition. When A is signaled, it reacquires the lock before proceeding. This is identical to condition variable semantics with an explicit mutex.

questions.q: A read-write lock (RW-lock) allows multiple readers but only one writer, and writers exclude all readers. When a writer is waiting, should new readers be allowed to acquire the lock?
options: Yes, new readers should enter; writer starvation is acceptable for throughput
No, new readers should be blocked to prevent writer starvation and ensure fairness
Readers and writers have equal priority, so it depends on the scheduling policy
This depends on whether the RW-lock uses phase-fair or preference semantics
questions.explanation: If readers are allowed while a writer waits, the writer may starve. Most RW-lock implementations block new readers when a writer is queued, giving writers preference. However, phase-fair variants use different strategies. The answer 'No' reflects the common anti-starvation approach; the fourth option hints at nuance but is too implementation-specific.

questions.q: A spinlock is a busy-wait lock that repeatedly checks a flag until it can acquire the lock. On a single-core CPU, what is a major drawback of a spinlock versus a blocking lock?
options: Spinlocks are faster and should always be preferred
On single-core, a spinning thread consumes CPU time while the lock holder is descheduled, causing wasteful busy-waiting and poor context-switch behavior
Spinlocks cannot protect shared data as effectively as blocking locks
Spinlocks are incompatible with interrupt handlers
questions.explanation: On a single-core system, if a thread spins while the lock holder is blocked or descheduled, the spinning thread wastes CPU cycles and delays the context switch needed to let the lock holder run. Blocking locks yield the CPU, allowing the lock holder to execute and release the lock. Spinlocks are better on multi-core systems where another core may hold the lock.

questions.q: A semaphore initialized to 0 is often used as a signaling mechanism. Thread A waits() on the semaphore while thread B calls signal(). When B calls signal(), what happens?
options: B's thread is blocked until another thread calls wait()
A wakes up immediately and continues execution; the semaphore count becomes 1 if A hasn't woken yet
The signal is lost if A has not called wait() before B signals
A and B both become eligible to run, but the scheduler decides which continues
questions.explanation: When B signals, the semaphore count increments from 0 to 1. If A is waiting, A wakes up and decrement the count back to 0. If A hasn't yet called wait(), the signal is stored as a count of 1, and A's subsequent wait() will immediately decrement and continue without blocking. Signals are not lost; they are stored in the count.

questions.q: In a monitor, a spurious wakeup occurs when a thread waiting on a condition variable wakes up even though no signal was called. What is the recommended way to handle this?
options: Use a try-finally block to ensure cleanup code runs
Always use a while loop instead of an if statement to re-check the condition after waking
Never use condition variables because spurious wakeups are unreliable
Catch spurious wakeups with a special exception handler
questions.explanation: Spurious wakeups are a real phenomenon in some systems (e.g., POSIX). The recommended pattern is to wrap the wait in a while loop that re-checks the condition after waking: `while (!condition) { cond_var.wait(lock); }`. This ensures that even if woken spuriously, the thread will re-enter wait if the condition is not satisfied. An if statement provides no re-check protection.

questions.q: A rendezvous barrier requires all N threads to reach it before any can proceed. If one thread never reaches the barrier, what happens?
options: The barrier times out and unblocks the other threads automatically
The N-1 waiting threads remain blocked indefinitely unless a timeout is implemented
The barrier merges waiting threads into a smaller group that can proceed
The operating system forcibly terminates the blocked threads
questions.explanation: A blocking rendezvous barrier has no inherent timeout. If one thread hangs or crashes before reaching the barrier, the other N-1 threads will wait forever. This is why production code using barriers should implement timeouts or monitors to detect hung threads. Some barrier implementations offer optional timeout parameters to mitigate this risk.
